expectations.text: Report covers all 9 areas in the Current State table, not just Hooks and MCP
expectations.evidence: Current State table lists all 9 areas: CLAUDE.md, Rules, Skills, Sub-agents, Hooks, Permissions, Settings, MCP, Features. Each has a status and finding count.

expectations.text: Every finding includes a Source line with a docs.anthropic.com URL
expectations.evidence: All 16 findings include a **Source**: line with a docs.anthropic.com URL. URLs reference /en/docs/memory, /en/docs/skills, /en/docs/sub-agents, /en/docs/hooks-guide, /en/docs/hooks, /en/docs/permissions, /en/docs/settings, and /en/docs/mcp.

expectations.text: Source citations include provenance markers: (via MCP) or (via Playwright CLI)
expectations.evidence: All 16 Source lines end with '(via MCP)'. No '(via Playwright CLI)' markers appear, which is expected since the baseline run did not use Playwright.
All 16 findings are tagged: 6 [good], 7 [improve], 3 [fix]. No untagged findings exist.

expectations.text: Report includes at least one finding about Hooks
expectations.evidence: Three Hooks findings: '[good] Notification hook is correctly configured', '[improve] SessionStart hook matcher is overly broad', '[improve] PostToolUse hook on Write|Edit runs a frontend quality gate globally'.

expectations.text: Report includes at least one finding about MCP
expectations.evidence: Two MCP findings: '[good] MCP servers are well-chosen and functional' (describes anthropic-docs, lighthouse, vercel servers), '[good] Anthropic docs MCP pre-approved in permissions'.

## Priority Actions section contains a 5-row table with columns #, Type, What to do, Why it matters. Actions cover stale permissions, missing CLAUDE.md, missing symlinks, missing SessionStart hook, and missing rules directory.

claims.claim: Report contains 16 distinct findings across all 9 areas
claims.evidence: Counted 16 findings: 3 under Instructions, 3 under Components, 7 under Automation, 3 under Integration. Current State table finding counts sum to 16 (2+1+2+1+3+2+2+2+1).

claims.claim: The report identifies that .claude/settings.local.json references non-existent minimal-framework paths
claims.evidence: Finding '[fix] Project-level permissions reference non-existent paths' identifies 4 stale permission rules pointing to /Users/giovannicordova/Documents/02_projects/minimal-framework/. This is a substantive, project-specific finding.

claims.claim: The report correctly identifies 5 of 6 skills are not symlinked
claims.evidence: Finding states 'only perspective is symlinked into ~/.claude/skills/' and the other five are not installed. This is a concrete, verifiable claim about the user's setup.

suggestions.assertion: Report covers all 9 areas in the Current State table, not just Hooks and MCP
suggestions.reason: This assertion checks table presence but not finding quality. A report could list all 9 areas with trivial or hallucinated findings and still pass. Consider adding an assertion that checks whether findings contain project-specific details (file paths, config values) rather than generic advice.

suggestions.assertion: Every finding includes a Source line with a docs.anthropic.com URL
suggestions.reason: This checks URL presence but not URL validity. The report cites docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/memory for CLAUDE.md guidance -- plausible but the actual page path may differ. A stronger eval would spot-check that at least one cited URL resolves to a page whose content matches the finding.

No assertion checks finding accuracy or specificity. This baseline run produced highly specific findings (e.g., exact file paths, concrete config values like 'startup|resume|clear|compact', specific plugin counts). An assertion like 'At least 3 findings reference specific file paths or config values from the audited project' would discriminate between substantive audits and generic boilerplate.

No assertion checks the total number of findings. The eval name is 'partial-area' suggesting a scenario where coverage might be limited, yet the baseline produced 16 findings across all 9 areas. If the eval is meant to test partial coverage, the expectations should verify that some areas are thin or missing -- otherwise the eval doesn't actually test the 'partial' scenario.

eval_feedback.overall: All 8 expectations pass. The assertions are well-structured for format compliance but lack depth on content quality. The baseline performed strongly -- 16 specific findings, full area coverage, consistent formatting -- which means these assertions don't discriminate between the with-skill and without-skill runs. Adding content-quality assertions (specificity, accuracy, actionability) would make the eval more diagnostic.
